question.text: If futures trade at fair value (F = S + C), an arbitrageur will:

answer.text: Not trade, as there is no arbitrage opportunity
Buy spot and sell futures for a guaranteed profit
Sell spot and buy futures for a guaranteed profit
Trade in the options market instead

explanation.text: **The Logic:** When futures = spot + cost of carry (fair value), there is no arbitrage opportunity. Buy-spot-sell-futures would only cover carrying costs with no profit. Arbitrage exists only when futures deviate from fair value. **The Trap:** At fair value, buying spot and selling futures nets exactly zero after accounting for carrying costs. Arbitrageurs need deviation from fair value to profit. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII covers cash-and-carry arbitrage conditions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Efficient markets keep futures near fair value — persistent deviations signal either market stress, illiquidity, or opportunity. Most arbitrage windows close within minutes.
